title: Genoa cake
subtitle: The British light fruit cake with glace cherries and citrus peel
excerpt: A firm, close-textured fruit cake studded with sultanas, glace cherries, and citrus peel. Lighter and less rich than a Christmas cake, Genoa cake is the kind of thing kept in a tin and sliced through the week.
sourceNotes: Genoa cake, despite its name, is a British rather than Italian product. It is documented in Mrs Beeton, Book of Household Management (1861), Project Gutenberg #10136, as a light fruit cake and appears throughout early twentieth-century British domestic baking literature. The glace cherry and citrus peel combination is the defining characteristic distinguishing it from a plainer sultana loaf.

recipe.freezeNotes: Wrap in baking paper and foil and freeze for up to two months.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: The cake keeps well for two weeks in a tin. Wrap in baking paper and store in a cool place.

glossaryTerms.definition: Cherries preserved in a thick sugar syrup, giving them a glossy appearance and a concentrated sweet flavour. Washing and drying them before use removes some surface syrup and helps them stay suspended in cake batter rather than sinking.

content.text: Genoa cake is a British fruit cake: close-crumbed, firm to slice, and generously fruited. It is lighter than a Christmas cake and keeps for two weeks in a tin without any maturing alcohol needed. The
are the visual signature; the citrus zest and almond extract give the flavour its character.

The cherries should be washed in warm water, dried thoroughly, and tossed in a spoonful of the recipe flour before being folded in. This removes the sticky syrup coating and gives the flour a surface to grip; both steps help prevent the cherries sinking to the base of the cake during baking.

Heat the oven to 150°C fan (170°C conventional). Double-line a 20 cm round tin with baking paper. Beat {{unsalted-butter}} and {{caster-sugar}} until pale and fluffy. Add {{eggs}} one at a time, beating well between each. Fold in {{plain-flour}} and {{baking-powder}}, then stir in {{sultanas}}, the zests from {{lemon}} and {{orange}}, and {{almond-extract}}. Spoon into the tin and level the surface. Scatter {{blanched-almonds}} over the top.

Bake for 80–85 minutes. After the first 40 minutes, if the almonds are colouring faster than the cake is baking, tent loosely with foil. Test with a skewer at 80 minutes; it should come out clean. An oven thermometer is useful for long bakes where an over-hot oven will burn the outside before the centre sets. Cool in the tin for 15 minutes, then on a rack.

If you add glacé cherries: rinse 100 g in warm water, dry them on kitchen paper, quarter them, and toss in a tablespoon of flour from the recipe before folding in with the sultanas. This reduces (but does not eliminate) the chance of sinking.
The cake slices cleanly when fully cold, not when still warm from the oven.
Replacing 50 g of the plain flour with ground almonds gives a moister, slightly denser result and a more pronounced almond note.
